title: Top Five Movie Dads
format: Sean and Amanda skip the guest slot up top and just talk fathers: dueling top-five movie-dad lists, then documentarian Kirsten Johnson (and her actual dad, on the phone) on 'Dick Johnson Is Dead.'

segments.heading: Movie news
segments.note: before the dad talk: a Lion King sequel, another Borat, and a movie about making 'The Godfather'

films.title: Borat
films.note: The original. Amanda wonders whether it's now a 2006 cultural artifact that doesn't speak to 2020 the way it once did.

films.title: Borat Subsequent Moviefilm
films.note: Sacha Baron Cohen's surprise sequel, arriving in 22 days, timed suspiciously close to his Oscar campaign for 'The Trial of the Chicago 7.'

films.title: The Trial of the Chicago 7
films.note: Cohen's other fall release, playing Abbie Hoffman; on the show's docket for a couple weeks out.

films.title: Irresistible
films.note: Jon Stewart's satire, which Amanda rated poorly on her brand-new Letterboxd profile, prompting the whole 'is Borat also dated now' question.

films.title: Francis and the Godfather
films.note: Barry Levinson's just-announced (not-yet-made) film about the Coppola/Robert Evans clash behind 'The Godfather,' with Oscar Isaac as Coppola and Jake Gyllenhaal as Evans.

segments.heading: Amanda's Top Five Movie Dads
segments.note: mostly fathers of daughters, ranked best to worst
segments.subject: Movie Dads

films.title: Father of the Bride
films.note: No. 1. George Banks (Steve Martin). A personal pick going back to watching it on VHS with her own dad around age eight; the airport phone call and the growing-up montage still get her every time.

films.title: 25th Hour
films.note: No. 2. James Brogan (Brian Cox). Her one pick that isn't about a daughter: the closing monologue, imagining a different life for his son, is the scene she can barely get through without crying.

films.title: A Star Is Born
films.note: No. 3. Lorenzo (Andrew Dice Clay), Ally's father and, unofficially, her manager. A deliberately funny inclusion given who's playing him, but the actual scene work is sincere.

films.title: Bad Boys II
films.note: No. 4. Marcus Burnett (Martin Lawrence), specifically the 'Who the fuck are you?' scene grilling his daughter Megan's date, Reggie. Protective, but also the one talking Will Smith's character down.

films.title: Easy A
films.note: No. 5. Dill Penderghast (Stanley Tucci), the permissive, unshockable dad to Olive (Emma Stone). Amanda calls him and Patricia Clarkson's character the movie's great underrated parents.

segments.heading: Sean's Top Five Movie Dads
segments.note: ranked in ascending order of goodness, bad dad to great dad, on purpose
segments.subject: Movie Dads

films.title: Boyz n the Hood
films.note: No. 1. Furious Styles (Laurence Fishburne), raising Tre (Cuba Gooding Jr.) with a clear-eyed political education most movie dads never attempt. Sean's pick for the closest thing to an ultimate movie dad.

films.title: Kramer vs. Kramer
films.note: No. 2. Ted Kramer (Dustin Hoffman), a single dad by necessity. Sean thinks the film has aged into something still honest about the day-to-day work of parenting, dated sexual politics and all.

films.title: Finding Nemo
films.note: No. 3. Marlin (Albert Brooks), a dad in peril searching for his lost son. Sean calls it the Bambi of its generation, tragic opening included.

films.title: National Lampoon's Vacation
films.note: No. 4. Clark Griswold (Chevy Chase), a bumbling, hopeful, occasionally felonious dad across the whole franchise. Sean rewatched all four over the preceding week. (Christmas Vacation was the one on repeat in Amanda's house growing up.)

films.title: The Royal Tenenbaums
films.note: No. 5. Royal Tenenbaum (Gene Hackman), the selfish-egotist type Sean reads as a filmmaker working out frustration with his own father. Paired as a bonus mention with Bernard Berkman (Jeff Daniels) in 'The Squid and the Whale,' a similar character from an Anderson collaborator.

picks.title: The Lion King
picks.note: The James Earl Jones original, pre-loaded on his honorable-mentions list before the Barry Jenkins sequel news even broke.

picks.title: Return of the Jedi
picks.note: Darth Vader, considered and passed over: 'not really investing the way he should,' redeemed only when he throws the Emperor down a shaft.

picks.title: Armageddon
picks.note: Honorable mention because he dies saving the world, though Amanda notes he also brings his daughter's boyfriend along on the mission that kills him.

picks.title: Mrs. Doubtfire
picks.note: The obvious omission both hosts pointedly disqualify: impersonating a nanny to see your kids during a divorce reads as deranged, not devoted.

picks.title: The Shining
picks.note: Jack Torrance, filed under destructive fathers, not really a contender.

picks.title: Jaws
picks.note: Martin Brody, the one she's wistful about cutting. Risks his life on the boat but is, at root, trying to protect his family.

picks.title: Moneyball
picks.note: A late add for divorced-dad etiquette: polite to the ex's new boyfriend, asks the right questions about a kid's first cell phone, moved to tears by a guitar-store performance.

picks.title: Sleepless in Seattle
picks.note: Considered for Tom Hanks, a dad also trying to find love again.

picks.title: The Pursuit of Happyness
picks.note: Considered for Will Smith, though she admits she didn't rewatch it in time and isn't sure she loves the film even if she loves what he does in it.

segments.heading: In conversation
segments.note: her dad even calls in partway through

films.title: Dick Johnson Is Dead
films.note: Her documentary about her father's dementia, staging his death (and resurrection) on camera repeatedly. New on Netflix the day of taping; uses slow motion in the fictional set pieces to stretch out her father's fleeting moments of lucidity.

films.title: Cameraperson
films.note: Her prior film, an archival essay built from decades of footage she shot for other documentaries. She says it taught her cinema could 'bring my mother back to life,' which led directly to this one.

films.title: The Father
films.note: Anthony Hopkins's dementia drama, which Sean saw back-to-back with 'Dick Johnson Is Dead' at Sundance. Both agree it's the more emotionally 'safe' version of the same subject: unlike Dick Johnson, Anthony Hopkins is still fine at the end.

Borat.title: Borat: Cultural Learnings of America for Make Benefit Glorious Nation of Kazakhstan

Borat Subsequent Moviefilm.title: Borat Subsequent Moviefilm: Delivery of Prodigious Bribe to American Regime for Make Benefit Once Glorious Nation of Kazakhstan
